planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; in zone 9 plant February–March for a May–June harvest and August for fall. Kabocha-type squashes including Sweet Mama are more heat-tolerant than most maxima types, making them better suited to zone 9 than Blue Hubbard or Buttercup. The compact vine is more manageable than sprawling maxima types, but still needs 5–8 feet of space per plant.

directions: Direct sow in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Sweet Mama is an All-America Selections-winning kabocha-type squash selected for exceptional sweetness and a compact vine habit. The small, flattened, dark green fruits (3–4 pounds) have brilliant deep-orange flesh with an almost impossibly sweet, nutty, dry flavor — more concentrated and dessert-like than butternut or acorn squash. When cut open the flesh is so dense and dry that it steams and bakes with a chestnut-like texture, and the sugar content is high enough to caramelize beautifully on cut surfaces. The thin edible skin softens when cooked, eliminating the need for peeling. Sweet Mama's superior flavor comes from its Japanese kabocha parentage, which Japanese breeders developed over decades specifically for sugar content and dry, flavorful flesh. In zone 9 harvest when the stem has dried and corked over and the skin has hardened — squeezing should produce no give in the flesh. Cure 2–4 weeks at room temperature before eating for best flavor development.
sources: All-America Selections — Sweet Mama Squash Award Record
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Kabocha Squash Types
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Winter Squash Varieties for Florida

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring; plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall. Elliott's Aster is naturally adapted to wet, seasonally flooded soils in coastal plain habitats from zone 7b–10 and tolerates both extended wet periods and seasonal drought once established. In zone 9 it blooms prolifically in October–November, one of the latest natives to flower, providing nectar at a critical time for migrating monarch butterflies.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained to periodically wet soil; Elliott's Aster is a native fall-blooming aster of the Southeastern Coastal Plain and Gulf Coast, producing spectacular masses of lavender to purple daisy flowers with yellow centers on 3–5 foot stems in October–November — among the latest native wildflowers to bloom in zone 9. The prolific late-season bloom covers the plant in hundreds of small flowers and provides critical nectar for migrating monarchs, native bees, and late-season pollinators when other nectar sources have shut down. Elliott's Aster is naturally adapted to the wet, poorly drained soils of coastal plain habitats, including wet edges of ponds, drainage ditches, and low spots in the landscape where most other ornamentals struggle. It tolerates brief flooding and heavy clay soils far better than most native asters. Cut back to 6 inches in late winter before new growth emerges; divide crowded clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Symphyotrichum elliottii
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Asters of Florida
North Carolina Botanical Garden — Coastal Plain Native Perennials

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil temperature reaches 65°F; in zone 9 plant April–June for a summer harvest. Black sesame requires the full warmth of the Gulf Coast summer to set and ripen seed pods. The plants are drought-tolerant once established and require no fertilization beyond initial soil preparation. Harvest when the lower pods begin to turn yellow-brown and before they split open and shatter — losing the seed. Cut the entire plant and hang upside down over a tarp to capture falling seeds.

directions: Direct sow thinly in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; black sesame is a color variant of common sesame producing jet-black seeds with a distinctly richer, nuttier, and more complex flavor than the pale white sesame seeds familiar from Western cooking — the higher pigment content is associated with higher concentrations of antioxidant sesamin, sesamolin, and lignans. Black sesame is the standard seed for traditional East and Southeast Asian preparations including black sesame paste (used in tang yuan rice balls and Asian pastries), black sesame ice cream, Japanese gomashio (sesame salt), Korean jook (sesame rice porridge), and black sesame halva. Toasted black sesame seeds have an almost smoky, deep nutty profile that white sesame cannot replicate. The slender, upright, ornamental plants with white-to-pink tubular flowers are attractive in the garden. Harvest by cutting and hanging entire plants over a cloth when pods begin to ripen — mature pods open at the top and shed seeds readily.
sources: University of Georgia Extension — Sesame Production in the Southeast
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Sesame Crop Profile

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after frost; in zone 9 plant March–May. Red Shrimp Plant is evergreen and root-hardy in zone 8b–11; in colder zone 8a it dies to the ground but recovers from roots if protected with mulch. It blooms year-round in zone 9 with the heaviest flowering in spring and fall. Prune hard in late winter to encourage dense new growth and prevent legginess.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, fertile soil; the Red Shrimp Plant produces distinctive pendulous spikes of overlapping salmon-red to copper-red bracts (modified leaves) that resemble a cooked shrimp in color, shape, and curve — with small white tubular true flowers emerging from between the bracts. The unique flower spikes appear continuously in zone 9 and are extraordinarily attractive to hummingbirds, who hover at each spike to probe for nectar through the entire growing season. The plant performs best in partial shade where it receives bright indirect light or morning sun with afternoon protection — full midday sun in zone 9 causes leaf scorching. It is more compact and floriferous in partial shade than in full sun. Cut back by half in late winter to maintain a bushy, productive habit; plants that are not pruned become tall and leggy with most flowering at the stem tips.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Justicia brandegeeana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Shrimp Plant for Texas Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Hummingbird-Attracting Plants for Louisiana

planting.note: Plant cormels or divisions after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Eddo taro is root-hardy in zone 9 and may overwinter in the ground with mulch protection, resuming growth in spring from the persistent corm. Plants grown in zone 9 can be left in the ground for multiple seasons, harvesting corms and cormels as needed without replanting.

directions: Plant cormels in moist, organically rich soil in partial shade to full sun with consistent moisture; Eddo (eddoe, eddo taro) is the small-corm type of Colocasia esculenta widely grown in the Caribbean, West Africa, and Southeast Asia for its edible corms and cormels — small, round to ovoid corms 1–2 inches across with hairy brown skin and white-to-purple-tinged starchy flesh. Unlike the large dasheen-type taro that produces a single massive central corm, eddo produces numerous small cormels clustered around a small central corm, making harvest easy — simply pull the plant and break apart the cormel cluster. The flesh is starchy and mild, with a slightly mucilaginous, creamier texture than dasheen, and is used in soups, stews, boiled as a side dish, and fried into chips throughout the African and Caribbean diaspora. The oxalic acid in raw taro requires thorough cooking; always boil or roast before eating. Eddo tolerates partial shade and moist soils and thrives in the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Edible Aroids for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Taro and Related Aroids
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Colocasia esculenta for Food Production
